excerpt_en: I joined a 9 AM radio calisthenics session in Central Park, invited by Mari. It seems to be a Japanese-led community. We did the Japanese version (Parts 1 and 2), then the English version. I joined because a man who helped spread Japanese food and sake in New York 30 years ago was there — Bon Yagi. I didn’t have much time, but I made the impression of being “the masu guy.” Afterward I was invited to run. I hadn’t planned to run and was carrying a backpack full of masu, but I ran anyway. I felt again how running helps you enter local communities. I’ll go again next week — in running gear.
body_en:
This time I’ll write about “the strength of running culture.”

Yesterday, Mari, whom I met, invited me

to join radio calisthenics at 9 AM.

And the place was Central Park!

I don’t know the details,

but it seemed like a Japanese-led community.

Encounters are once in a lifetime.

These opportunities are precious.

We did the Japanese version of radio calisthenics,

Parts 1 and 2,

and then did the English version once more.

Some Americans participated too.

I joined for a reason:

I heard that someone who helped spread Japanese food and sake in New York about 30 years ago would be there,

and I really wanted to meet him.

It was Bon Yagi.

He’s a person who works hard in New York.

I run a masu brand,

so I got to meet him.

I didn’t have much time,

so we couldn’t talk in detail, but

I was able to make the impression that I’m “the masu guy.”

After radio calisthenics,

I was invited: “We’re going to run next — want to join?”

There were already lots of people running in Central Park,

so I decided to run too, since it was a rare chance.

I didn’t think I’d be running, so

I wasn’t wearing workout clothes,

and I had a backpack full of masu,

so I couldn’t put it down anywhere,

and ended up running with it on my back. Lol

Because I had run in America several times

to prepare for the Medoc Marathon,

I was somehow able to run a full lap even with luggage. Lol

Still, running without proper gear is tough!

But thanks to that,

I was able to connect with people,

so I once again felt that if you can run in any country,

it’s easier to enter the local community.

I’ve already experienced this in Japan and Taiwan,

and in America too, I felt running is a communication tool,

a place where community forms, and a very strong culture.

They do radio calisthenics & running every Sunday,

so I’ll have one more chance,

and I plan to go again.

Next time, in running gear!! Lol

And for lunch, I went to McDonald’s nearby.

McDonald’s is relatively cheap,

and they accept credit cards, so it’s convenient.

Why?

I only have $13 in cash left. Lol

I want to save my cash for emergencies,

so I want to use places that take cards. Lol

Also, other restaurants are expensive.

To be honest, even the cheapest McDonald’s set

is about 1,000 yen.

For solo lunch,

I don’t need to be fancy,

so this is fine for me!

I know it’s not great health-wise,

but when I calculate in yen,

my frugal habit makes it hard to spend on meals just for myself.

I’d rather use that money for FOMUS activities,

and even just taking the subway in New York costs about 500 yen,

so moving around costs 1,000 yen round trip.

It’s tough. Lol

Anyway, it was a day that reminded me how strong running culture is!

excerpt_en: My three days with Chris are over. Couchsurfing lets me experience things I couldn’t with hotels, and it connects me with locals while saving money, though I still have to be considerate. That’s why I alternate Couchsurfing and hotels in New York. Today I stayed near Central Park in a capsule-style hotel, walked around, and bought pasta, tomato sauce, and muffins to save money. At night I watched the Broadway musical CHICAGO. The ticket was about 18,000 yen for a second-floor side seat, and the theater was packed. I couldn’t catch all the English, but I understood the story since I studied beforehand, and the singing and dancing were incredible. Then I visited Times Square at night.
body_en:
Three days with Chris are over

My time with Chris, who welcomed me from the day I arrived in New York,

is now over!

We both had our own activities,

so we didn’t go out together much,

but we ate meals, went on a picnic,

and talked at night,

so it was a really fun time.

Couchsurfing lets you experience things

that are hard to get on a normal trip or hotel stay,

so it’s very valuable.

Also, if I did this after turning 40,

it would be tough both physically and mentally,

so I’m glad I experienced it in my 20s.

And for expanding the FOMUS brand,

it connects me with locals,

so this method was absolutely the right one.

Plus, there’s no lodging cost. Lol

It’s all good, but

I’m staying in someone else’s house,

so I do have to be considerate.

That’s why my New York schedule is:

1) Couchsurfing 3 nights

2) Hotel 1 night

3) Couchsurfing 3 nights

4) Hotel 1 night

5) Couchsurfing 2 nights

That’s the schedule! Lol

If I’m out late at night,

with Couchsurfing I’d worry about the host,

so it’s difficult.

It’s hard to schedule nighttime events.

So today, I’m at a hotel.

Near Central Park,

in a hotel that feels like a capsule hotel.

It’s actually comfortable in its own way.

I arrived in the morning,

walked around Central Park a bit,

and stopped by a supermarket.

I thought it was time to start cooking for myself.

Outside Japan, everything is expensive,

so if you eat out every day for lunch,

your money just disappears.

And since I’m alone,

that lunch is “consumption,”

not “investment.”

So, based on my address-hopper experience,

I bought what I feel is the best option:

pasta and tomato sauce!

And muffins because they were relatively cheap.

That cost about 1,200 yen.

The sauce is for one day,

but the pasta is for three meals,

and the muffins are one per meal,

so it saves money.

So, cooking!

Well, just boiling pasta. Lol

I’d like to add meat,

basil, or cheese, but

with constant moving,

I can’t buy refrigerated items,

and I can’t increase my luggage,

so I only use what I can finish.

And it’s just one night at the hotel,

so this is literally it.

I’ve lost all interest in “meals for one,”

so you might think,

“You’re in America, isn’t this a waste?”

But I’m here for work,

so sightseeing and food are second or third priority.

Of course, when I eat with someone,

I enjoy it fully!

Broadway musical “CHICAGO”

When you think of New York,

it’s Broadway, right!!

This trip is about expanding the FOMUS brand

and experiencing top-tier entertainment,

so I’ll spend money on these things.

The ticket was about 18,000 yen.

Even with that, it was a second-floor side seat,

so it’s like the second-lowest grade.

The venue was packed.

And this happens every day—

it’s an insane business.

CHICAGO is a super long-running show,

it’s been going for over 20 years...

Musicals have songs, dancing, and dialogue,

and of course, it’s all in English.

Honestly, with my current English,

I couldn’t understand it perfectly,

and not being able to laugh when everyone else did

made me a little sad.

But I studied the story beforehand,

so I could follow the outline like,

“Ah, this scene is about this...”

I understood the rough story.

Still, I want to enjoy the dialogue too.

I need to master English fast!!

The singing and dancing were,

as expected of professionals, incredible.

CHICAGO’s story is quite intense,

but it also had tons of comedy,

so the show was full of laughs from start to finish!

There weren’t many stage changes like Cirque du Soleil, though.

I actually know a bit about Broadway systems,

and for CHICAGO, there was pro music involved,

and I knew some union behind-the-scenes,

so I watched with those things in mind too.

Conclusion: CHICAGO was amazing!!

Times Square at night

After that, I went to Times Square.

It was my first time there at night,

and even after 9 PM there were tons of people,

and maybe because of soccer,

there were lots of people wearing Argentina jerseys.

My stay in America is down to one week.

I’ll do everything I can, full force!

excerpt_en: I had a Masu Photo shoot in the morning with Mizuki, a Japanese-American who’s lived in the U.S. since age one. We talked a lot during the shoot. After that, I moved my base to North Elizabeth, New Jersey, to stay with my Couchsurfing host Jimmy for three days. It saves about 35,000 yen compared to New York hotel prices (as of 2024/07/09), and lets me experience local life. I bought cup ramen and pasta sauce, and Jimmy cooked vegetable stir-fry while I shared miso soup. In the U.S., proactive communication is essential — especially when you’re staying in someone’s home — so I push myself to talk even when my English isn’t perfect.
body_en:
Masu Photo shoot

So, today I had a Masu Photo shoot in the morning.

From 9:30,

the meeting point was a park near my hotel.

Today’s model was Mizuki.

She’s half Japanese and American,

and has lived in the U.S. since she was one.

She uses Japanese when talking to her mother,

so she was fluent,

and I ended up speaking Japanese too. Lol

She used to be a serious dancer,

now she does nail-related work,

she’s an artist,

and we talked about a lot while shooting. ✨

This was my second shoot in New York!

I’m truly grateful that people join Masu Photo events like this!

Shifting my base to New Jersey

For the next three days, my base

is not New York but North Elizabeth.

That’s a different state — New Jersey.

Still, it’s easy by train!!

It takes just over an hour, but

because you’re crossing state lines,

the ticket is $8!

Plus the usual transit costs about $2,

so transportation alone is over 1,600 yen.

Those small costs really add up...

Why New Jersey? Couchsurfing.

My host Jimmy, who lets me stay for three days,

lives in North Elizabeth.

As of 2024/07/09,

the cheapest New York hotels are still $75.

That’s over 10,000 yen.

So just staying three nights saves

about 35,000 yen in lodging costs.

Plus, I can experience local daily life,

so it’s a really good experience.

When I got to the station,

Jimmy picked me up.

He showed me the house,

and gave me the basic instructions.

*The room I’m staying in from today

He had his own plans,

so I went to a nearby supermarket he told me about.

Since it’s not downtown New York,

I hoped things might be cheaper,

but they weren’t. Lol

I bought cup ramen and pasta sauce

for about 1,200 yen.

Even if I save on lodging,

food and transport costs are unavoidable,

so I try to be as frugal as possible.

At night, Jimmy made vegetable stir-fry

and a plate of vegetables and cheese, and I ate that.

I also shared miso soup packets I brought from Japan.

“Miso soup” is a Japanese staple!

We ate together.

Talk even if your English isn’t perfect

Something I’ve realized in America is that,

unlike Japanese people,

talking a lot is critical for communication.

Japanese people tend to be quiet,

whether they can speak English or not.

That’s fine between Japanese people,

but if the other person is from another country, it changes.

I’ve Couchsurfed with four hosts so far in America, including Jimmy,

and I always try to be proactive.

Honestly,

I don’t understand all the English.

But the attitude of truly listening

and truly trying to speak

does get through.

That’s very important—

especially when you’re staying in someone’s home,

communication is everything!!!

Greetings are essential in business, but

this is even more important because you’re sharing private time.

If I don’t use 120% of my communication skills,

I can’t stand on the same ground as the other person.

That’s how important communication is here.

This is my first time living in an American person’s house (and we just met),

but it’s so stimulating that

every day is interesting!

I’m glad I did two years as an address hopper in Japan. Lol

excerpt_en: It was my second day in New Jersey, but I still went into Manhattan. I decided to watch another Broadway musical and booked Moulin Rouge the day before. I chose it because Nishino (King Kong Nishino) had mentioned it when he challenged Broadway, and I wanted to see the lighting and costumes. The venue’s red lighting was stunning. I watched from the first floor — I like seeing faces up close. I couldn’t catch all the English, but the singing and dancing were fantastic, and it made me love musicals even more. Public transportation in the U.S. still feels inconvenient compared to Japan.
body_en:
Travel is tough

It’s my second day after arriving in New Jersey,

but I still went into Manhattan during the day.

I didn’t have any shoots scheduled,

so I thought I’d explore around Elizabeth,

but my host Jimmy said, “Do whatever you want,”

so I decided to watch another Broadway musical

and booked it yesterday.

I booked “Moulin Rouge.”

Many of you probably know it, right?

I only knew the name, but

as usual, I checked the synopsis and story in advance.

Preparation is essential!

So,

I spent about 2,500 yen round trip

to go from Elizabeth to Broadway.

Moulin Rouge

This time, I watched the 2 PM show.

On some days there are 2 PM and 8 PM shows.

It was a little cheaper than CHICAGO, around 10,000 yen.

And it was a first-floor seat.

When entering the theater, there’s the usual bag check.

I only had water and masu,

so it was a quick pass.

Then I went inside.

The view inside the venue

was a stylish space with deep red lighting.

Why did I choose Moulin Rouge?

Because when King Kong Nishino challenged Broadway,

he talked about Moulin Rouge,

and I had heard about the lighting,

so I wanted to see the space in person.

Also, it won awards for costumes,

so I was very interested.

Seeing it in person, it was powerful.

I had a little time, so I went up to the second floor too.

The view might be better from up top,

but personally I prefer the first floor where you can see faces clearly.

I watched from the right side of the first floor.

This height and this impact.

It was another love story,

and I couldn’t catch most of the English dialogue,

but I really enjoyed the dance and songs.

Also, I simply started to like musicals more.

Such fun entertainment happens every day on Broadway,

and it’s an amazing business model.

Honestly, I don’t think I’ll try to do anything on Broadway myself,

but seeing people who challenge it in Japan and America,

I’m reminded how incredible it is.

If you come to America,

Broadway musicals are a must!

Public transportation isn’t great

Overall, I feel U.S. public transportation is weak.

Some train stations don’t even have elevators,

so you have to lift your suitcase.

Also, train seats aren’t soft like in Japan—

they’re like plastic.

So they’re less comfortable than in Japan.

Today, trains were delayed both when going from New York to Elizabeth and back,

so I had to wait a lot,

and you don’t know which platform it departs from until the last minute...

In that sense, Japan is amazing.

I’ve already been here 19 days,

so I can make comparisons.

Honestly, I’m not here as a tourist,

so I haven’t gone to many tourist spots. Lol

I’ll do that when I go with someone.

Will I go see the Statue of Liberty on this trip? Lol

excerpt_en: I stayed in all day to do desk work and online meetings. Recently I’d been out so much that I couldn’t get my own tasks done, so I used a full day in New York for admin work. The Japan–New York time difference (about 13 hours) makes scheduling meetings difficult — midnight meetings happen often. I cooked pasta with tomato sauce (no salt available) and ate a muffin. I set aside this desk-work day because the schedule gets packed starting tomorrow, and one of the hardest tasks is searching for models on Instagram.
body_en:
Today I stayed inside all day,

doing desk work and online meetings.

Recently I’d been active outside a lot,

and I couldn’t really work on my own tasks.

So I decided to knock it all out,

and used a full day of my New York stay for admin work.

I’m also running multiple projects in Japan,

so I need meetings for those too,

and the time difference becomes an issue.

Japan and New York are about 13 hours apart,

so matching schedules is a bit difficult.

One downside of working between Japan and America

is probably this time difference.

Midnight meetings happen often. Lol

Since I didn’t go out,

I cooked for myself.

I used pasta and tomato sauce I bought at the supermarket.

But there was no salt in the house I’m staying at,

so I boiled it without salt. Lol

The tomato sauce is for pasta,

so it had some saltiness, and it was fine.

And I had one of the 4 muffins I bought.

By the way, the muffins were $6.

About 1,000 yen.

At 250 yen each,

that’s relatively cheap for America.

So yeah,

I’m living a very unglamorous New York life. Lol

Tomorrow is busy

That said, my schedule is packed from tomorrow,

so I set aside today for desk work.

I probably won’t have much time to work otherwise.

I’ve valued time management since Japan,

but my 20s are running out fast for me.

I have to give it my all now,

so that in my 30s I can fully use my skills.

For that,

I face desk work head-on,

even though I don’t really want to.

One of those tasks is

searching for models.

Just endlessly finding models on Instagram.

It’s exhausting.

It might actually be the hardest work,

even if it looks like I’m just playing. 😂

excerpt_en: I woke up at 5 AM and went to a lake in New Jersey with my host Jimmy. We swam at 6:30, but I turned back early because I only had a swimsuit. A staff member asked if we had permission; the police were called, but it wasn’t a big deal. I rushed back to catch the 9 AM train to Manhattan, stayed at a hotel for the day, and did a Masu Photo shoot with Shary, a commercial model. In the afternoon I went to the New York Asian Film Festival to watch “Bottle George” (based on King Kong Nishino’s work) and met Nishino. Later I toured art galleries in Chelsea for inspiration for the Sagishima art gallery, and ended the day at a Japanese-owned bar recommended by Teeny.
body_en:
I woke up at 5 AM,

and drove about 30 minutes with my host Jimmy

to a lake in New Jersey to swim!

We decided this yesterday evening,

and even though I had a shoot scheduled from the morning,

we figured early morning would work,

so we got in the water at 6:30.

His friend Kevin joined too!

They told us to swim to a goal far from the shore,

but they had real swim suits and goggles,

and I only had a swimsuit,

so I swam partway and realized I wouldn’t last,

and turned back.

I can swim, but

swimming in a lake instead of the ocean was actually harder.

So I waited on the shore first.

That was fine, but

a staff-like person came and asked,

“Do you have permission to use the lake?”

I had no idea, so I said,

“I don’t know.”

They were swimming far away,

so the staff’s attention was on me.

Then they said they’d call the police.

“Wait, that’s how it goes? Lol”

After they finished swimming,

the staff and police talked, but

apparently if you come 10 times,

the police get called about 3 times,

according to Jimmy’s friend. Lol

The police were smiling,

so it probably wasn’t a big deal.

I never thought I’d have the police called on me in America.

That’s a good memory too. Lol

We got back home by 8:30.

I immediately showered and got ready,

because I had to catch the 9 AM train

or I’d miss the next one!

Back to a Manhattan hotel

I took the train for about an hour

and returned to Manhattan.

Today only, I’m staying at a hotel.

*So I can come back anytime

*So I don’t bother the host

I checked in and went to the room.

After this, I have a Masu Photo shoot.

Masu Photo shoot

This time, the shoot was with Shary,

a commercial model in New York.

We shot at a park near the hotel.

America is full of many nationalities and ethnicities,

so the shoots are diverse,

and I enjoy shooting with people I could never meet in Japan,

and I’ve confirmed that the FOMUS brand is accepted here too.

This U.S. trip has been very stimulating.

To the New York Asian Film Festival

In the afternoon, I went to Lincoln Center.

The purpose was to watch

“Bottle George,” based on King Kong Nishino’s work,

which was screened at the New York Asian Film Festival.

Nishino was in New York at the time,

so I got to talk with him again for the first time since the May event.

There were 6–7 works screened, but

honestly, Bottle George was overwhelmingly the highest quality.

That’s not favoritism.

Or rather,

this might get me in trouble, but

I felt like I could make a film at that level. Lol

FOMUS plans to make animation in the near future,

and I want to create works that get nominated for awards like this.

Anyway, Bottle George was amazing.

I want to watch it a few more times. Lol

Art gallery hopping

After the film festival,

I walked about 40 minutes

to Chelsea.

I heard that this area

has tons of art galleries lined up.

Unlike museums,

you can see them for free.

I visited these galleries to get ideas

for the FOMUS art gallery currently being built on Sagishima in Hiroshima.

Thinking about display methods

and colors behind the works

is my role for the FOMUS art gallery design.

That said,

it’s a container-based gallery,

so it’s small,

not what most people imagine.

That’s why it has to be simple, stylish,

and a place everyone wants to visit — a big responsibility.

I need to see the art capital,

steal the good ideas,

and share them with the team building the gallery.

“Japan-ness” is covered by masu,

so it’s about balancing the rest,

or maybe fully leaning in without worrying about balance.

The worries never disappear,

but the excitement is even stronger.

To a Japanese-owned bar

Teeny, who helped me in Los Angeles,

recommended a bar in New York,

so I went to check it out.

It’s behind a salon,

so it’s a real hidden spot.

And the atmosphere is great.

I came around 8 PM,

and it was already packed.

It’s doing very well.

This place serves sake and sake cocktails,

so I came to study.

I’m not sure if FOMUS will open a restaurant,

but I want to do bar events,

so it’s important to learn from popular places in New York.

I’ll pay 3,200 yen for one cocktail as tuition. Lol

It’s not that the bar is ripping people off —

it’s the high New York prices plus the weak yen.

The bar runs with three people:

a Japanese owner and two local staff.

They’re not just pouring sake —

they’re making cocktails,

so it takes time.

As for the taste,

honestly, it’s normal, but

for people overseas, this style is appealing.

If we served sake and liqueur in FOMUS masu,

as masu cocktails,

I feel like it could be pretty good.

Though the square shape makes it hard to drink. Lol

So yeah,

with art and bars,

to grow the FOMUS brand,

I need to know those worlds myself,

or I can’t even get close to them,

so I’m investing money and time.

This isn’t showing off how hard I’m working —

it’s just me thinking about how to compete overseas

in an era where overseas feels farther away.

Also, movies aside,

if you go to a bar alone,

everyone around is with friends or couples,

so you stand out a lot.

It worked in Los Angeles, but

in New York,

I strongly feel it would be more fun to make friends to drink with.

excerpt_en: My last Couchsurfing stay in America was with Dan, in a high-end apartment with a lobby and doorman. I slept on the living room sofa and focused on proactive communication, using translation apps when needed. Dan took me to Rockefeller Center and even covered the admission. Then we went to Brooklyn Bridge, had drinks at a bar, and ate Thai food. Later we went to a rooftop bar. Spending time with locals changes what you can see, and Couchsurfing really shines there.
body_en:
My last Couchsurfing stay in America

My New York stay is almost over.

I checked out of the hotel

and headed to Dan’s place, my host from today.

Probably the most luxurious Couchsurfing spot of this U.S. trip.

When I got there,

it was a secure apartment with a lobby

and a doorman.

I couldn’t enter alone,

so Dan came to the lobby to pick me up.

This time, the place was a room in a luxury apartment.

I slept on the sofa in the living room.

I chatted with Dan,

and we talked about our activities.

Of course, communication was in English.

From my experiences so far,

being proactive makes the other person happy,

so I talked actively.

Honestly, when I don’t know words,

I use a translation app,

and just talk anyway.

There’s an image abroad that “Japanese are shy,”

so I try to show a strong attitude like,

“I’m different. I’m open.”

*That just gets you to normal level.

Then he asked what places I’d visited,

and after I answered,

he said there was a recommended spot

and took me there.

It was Rockefeller Center.

I knew about it, but

it’s like climbing Tokyo Skytree alone in Japan,

so I didn’t think I’d go.

But suddenly, the chance appeared! Lol

And Dan even paid the admission.

*Huge thanks!!

You can see all of America’s scenery from here,

Rockefeller.

They say the views are totally different in daytime and night,

but this time it was daytime.

It’s not a place I would come alone,

so I was really grateful he brought me here.

Next, we took the subway

to the Brooklyn Bridge.

This bridge is also a famous tourist spot.

Honestly, it was so hot that I don’t remember much. Lol

After that,

Dan and I toasted at a nearby bar. 🥂

It was so hot outside,

so just being inside was a relief.

Dan treated me here too.

I keep accepting his kindness.

Then,

we went to a Thai restaurant.

I had told Dan beforehand,

when he asked, “What do you want to eat?”

that I wanted Thai food, so he chose it.

I ordered fried rice.

It doesn’t look great,

but the taste was amazing!!

Then we walked a bit

to a recommended rooftop bar.

I didn’t take photos, but

the female staff were all very beautiful.

It was too busy,

so there was zero chance for Masu Photo,

but it was a great experience.

The world looks different with locals

This U.S. trip is solo,

so I’m really grateful when people invite me out for drinks.

As I wrote before,

in America, it seems common to drink with friends or couples,

so it’s hard to enter places alone,

and every place is busy, so it’s hard to talk.

That’s why being with someone

makes everything more fun,

and since they’re locals,

they know New York inside out!

Couchsurfing is about connecting with locals,

and after experiencing it,

I felt again how amazing this service is

to share such fun time.

excerpt_en: A week later, I joined radio calisthenics again and ran in Central Park, chatting with participants. I ran about 5.5 km and then ran to Times Square. Later I went bar hopping with Dan — a rooftop bar, an Irish pub to watch the Spain vs England final, and another rooftop bar. I visited St. Patrick’s Cathedral, ate Thai pineapple fried rice for $20, and took photos at Grand Central with the masu camera. Back home, I chatted with Dan in English and felt I was getting more comfortable, though I still need to study.
body_en:
After a week!

It’s Sunday.

I joined radio calisthenics again, like last week.

We did Japanese radio calisthenics and the overseas version.

About 8 people moved our bodies.

After that, we went running.

The venue is inside Central Park,

so there’s always someone running.

People who train themselves

have amazing bodies.

This has nothing to do with gender.

American eating habits are pretty intense,

so if you do nothing, you gain weight easily.

That’s why, to live a healthy life,

many people run here.

There weren’t many marathon runners today,

but running inside Central Park was really fun.

While running,

I talked with participants,

and that was interesting too.

Everyone has their own big background

for being in New York.

People who’ve only been in New York about 10 days

don’t often join communities like this, apparently,

but I used my communication skills

and the ability to naturally join the circle.

Outside Japan,

a bit of boldness seems just right.

So I ran about 5.5 km and we disbanded.

Then I ran to Times Square.

Bar hopping

I went back to Dan’s place, took a shower,

then went to a rooftop bar with him.

He took me to a stylish, not-too-loud bar.

Next we went to an Irish pub.

It was the Spain vs England soccer final,

so we watched the match.

Watching soccer at an Irish pub

was a first for me.

It’s not something I could easily do alone,

so having someone with me felt reassuring.

Soccer viewing

After that, he took me to another rooftop bar.

Then we split up for a bit,

and I went to St. Patrick’s Cathedral.

It was amazing!!

Highly recommended if you’re sightseeing!

After that, I was hungry, so

I went to Thai food.

I ordered pineapple fried rice.

I was the only solo diner, of course.

That was $20.

So about 3,200 yen.

In Thailand it’d be under 1,000 yen.

New York is really expensive.

Then, I went to Grand Central Station,

and took photos with the masu camera.

I got good photos.

After getting home,

I talked with Dan.

Of course the conversation was in English,

but I’m getting used to it

and can actually enjoy it.

Still, my English isn’t strong enough yet,

so I want to keep studying.

excerpt_en: As the title says, my activities in America ended today. I did a Masu Photo shoot in the morning; the model was 2 hours late, so I used the time for other work and kept the shoot short. After that I went to MoMA to see works by Picasso and Monet, and it felt like my NY trip started with art and ended with art. I picked up my luggage from Dan’s, headed to the airport 4 hours early, and flew Level Airlines. I tried to use Priority Pass at the lounge, but my terminal didn’t accept it, so I used it at a restaurant instead for a $28 credit and got a $27 burger and a $13 beer. This concluded my U.S. trip (started June 21). I learned I need longer stays to really enter communities and that leading with “the person” is key to expanding the brand. Next: 12 days in Spain.
body_en:
As the title says,

my activities in America end today!

On this final day,

I did a Masu Photo shoot in the morning.

The model was two hours late,

so I shifted that time to other tasks.

But I had plans after this,

so I kept the shoot short.

*Max 60 minutes per person

*Time is important

In my case,

I finish shooting at an incredible speed,

so it’s really fast.

So I devoted most of the time to conversation.

Because getting people to know the FOMUS brand

is the most important thing.

To the Museum of Modern Art

And the last destination in America was

the Museum of Modern Art.

It has works by many famous painters,

so it’s a great place to see art.

There were works by Picasso and Monet too.

My first day in New York was the Met,

and my last day was MoMA.

A New York trip that started with art

and ended with art. Lol

Then I went to Dan’s house,

where I had left my luggage,

chatted a bit,

and then headed out.

I absolutely couldn’t be late this time,

so I arrived about 4 hours early.

I checked my luggage and went through security.

The line was pretty long.

For the first time in my life, I used a Spanish airline called LEVEL.

Probably I was the only Japanese person on the flight. Lol

Using Priority Pass

This flight was 7 hours,

and I didn’t book any food plan on the plane,

so I needed to eat beforehand.

I got a Priority Pass in Japan this time

and wanted to try it,

so I went to an airport lounge, but

the lounge in my terminal didn’t accept Priority Pass.

Apparently it works in Terminal 4 at JFK,

but I was departing from Terminal 8.

So I checked the Priority Pass app,

and there was one restaurant I could use, so I went.

It was like a steakhouse.

I wasn’t sure if it would work,

so I asked the staff,

they scanned my Priority Pass and said,

“You have $28 worth of credit.”

That means a discount of about 4,500 yen with Priority Pass!

So I ordered a $27 burger.

That alone is basically free, but

I had a bunch of small dollar bills left and wanted to use them up,

so I ordered a beer.

That was $13 — about 2,600 yen. Lol

Burgers and beer are the best combo.

The total after tax and tip was $15.5.

The $28 discount was super helpful.

This was my first time using Priority Pass,

and I felt I could enjoy it more if I use it well.

So,

the U.S. trip that started on June 21

ended safely here!

Honestly,

there were lots of things to reflect on.

I wanted to take more Masu Photos,

but I couldn’t gather enough models, etc.

This part is different from Southeast Asia,

so it was difficult.

Also, I realized that about 10 days in one city

isn’t enough to really break in

and sell the FOMUS brand.

You really need to stay for at least two months,

and enter the community,

get people to know me,

and make them feel, “I want to do something fun with Massu.”

That feels most important.

Rather than pushing masu or FOMUS,

you have to go with “the person” first,

or the brand won’t expand.

This U.S. trip made me feel all of that.

Next is 12 days in Spain.

Even in this short time,

I’ll do everything I can.

Honestly, I have no connections.

How do I struggle through that?

I’m writing this article on the flight to Barcelona,

and I can’t predict anything — that’s the reality.

Still, I’ll value each day,

make sure more people know the FOMUS brand,

drop unnecessary pride, run through it,

and move on to the main Ireland match!

excerpt_en: 7/16: I arrived in Barcelona after about a 7-hour flight. Because I enjoyed New York all day, I slept well and the flight felt short. I reached at 1 PM local time and headed to my hotel for the next 5 nights. My nearest station is Sagrada Familia; I chose a hotel within 5 minutes on foot to make shooting easier. Five nights cost about 42,000 yen (8,000 per night), which is fine for this location. Coming out of the subway, I saw Sagrada Familia — a long-time dream to see it before completion. Then I walked the city, checked prices (cheaper than New York), and treated myself to tapas, including salad, ajillo-like dish, and paella.
body_en:
7/16

I arrived in Barcelona!!

It was about a 7-hour flight, but

maybe because I enjoyed New York from morning to night,

I slept well and it felt quick!

With the time difference,

I arrived at 1 PM.

For now,

I’m heading to the hotel where I’ll stay for 5 nights.

I’ve moved through four cities so far,

and every place has different transport rules,

so it’s a learning experience each time.

Still, I’m used to it now,

and made it to the nearest station by train transfers.

The nearest station is “Sagrada Familia Station.”

Yes, I booked a place within a 5-minute walk of Sagrada Familia.

I could have used Couchsurfing, but

I wanted to shoot with Sagrada Familia in the background,

and considering movement and key issues with hosts,

I chose a hotel so I can move easily

and sleep until just before shoot time.

Even so, 5 nights cost about 42,000 yen.

That’s 8,000 yen per night.

Given how close it is to Sagrada Familia,

if it’s under 10,000 yen, it’s OK!

So when you come up from underground, what you see is...

Yes, Sagrada Familia!

Finally!!

I wanted to see it before completion,

so I somehow made it in time. ✨

  • Stroll

After checking in at the hotel,

I went for a walk first.

I wanted to check prices, etc.

Compared to New York, it’s relatively cheap!

Since I’m in Barcelona,

I treated myself only for the first night’s dinner.

Especially because I hadn’t eaten since the New York burger — about 12 hours. Lol

Spain is famous for “tapas,”

so I went to a well-rated spot.

*Tapas are a type of Spanish cuisine, small plates or appetizers.

Originally, they were simple foods used to cover drinks (“tapa”),

and are commonly served at bars.

Tapas are very diverse, from cold items (olives, cheese, ham)

to hot items (croquetas, patatas bravas, gambas al ajillo, etc.).

Typically you order several tapas at once and share them with friends or family,

enjoying a variety of flavors.

It’s also popular to go “tapas hopping” (Tapas Crawl),

visiting multiple places and eating along the way. (Renfe) (Rail Europe).

Tapas symbolize Spanish culture and the enjoyment of food,

and play an important role in socializing through meals.

So, here’s what I ordered.

Salad, something like ajillo, and paella.

And it was pretty filling!!

Of course, I came alone.

#Sharing is the best part, but

I had no one to share with, so

I just ate it all myself!! Lol

Even so, I had a bit of salad left,

so I took it to go.

Now, the next few days

will be activities in Barcelona!!

Look forward to it!
